speaker: interviewer
text: So you ran to the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Yes. And so then again, you know, after-- then people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was somebody there with you under the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was-- there was-- there were some other people, yes. I don't remember how many. And I remember where I saw some people are-- took off their jackets. And they threw it up on the air so they can see that we are not soldiers, you know? And apparently, they-- they did notice that-- and they stopped. They stopped the shooting. And they went away. And everybody, you know, again, they took the dead and the wounded, threw them in in a boxcar. And-- and the others went into their car. And so again, we're still going, still traveling-- we don't know where. And a couple days later, again, the train stops. The train stops. They all-- you know, they open up the-- the box, you know-- open up the boxcars. And, you know, we look around. And we even dared to go down. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we see-- we see-- we used to have some young soldiers and older soldiers. And we noticed that the young ones disappeared. And we were left with the older ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, German soldiers. And they-- they were not so strict. They didn't yell at us-- get back to the car, get back to the car. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a feeling. We had a feeling that it's getting very close to the end of the war. And a lot of people, even slowly, got out of where the train is, into-- into the village. Well, anyway-- Did you think of doing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, as a matter of fact, I did. And all of a sudden, I see a truck. So I came to a tree. And I was hiding behind the tree. I saw it was a military truck. But you know, I thought it was the Germans. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] The truck stops. I saw-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --that they were different. You know, they weren't-- they weren't Germans. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was the Americans.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we told them about, you know, that there's a train with a lot of people.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, go down there a little further, and you will see a lot of American soldiers. And they will give you food.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went down there. And then later on, a lot of other people from there went there. But what did they-- what they had to give us is canned food-- beef, you know. And our stomach was not used to food or greasy food. And a lot of people died. A lot of people died right after the liberation, because they couldn't-- they couldn't-- they couldn't take-- you know, they ate too much or whatever, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- And you? What happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I ate, but-- but you know, I didn't get-- I didn't get sick. I didn't get sick. And we go, and we don't-- we still don't know what to do. We are running around-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --I should say, like animals. Were you with anybody special?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, nobody special. But we-- I got together with some other people, and we walked together. And what they did is they rounded us up, little by little. And they took us to this camp. They make-- they made out of the Hitler Youth Camp, they made a displaced-persons camp at a place called Feldafing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is this now?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- that was about the beginning of May.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945. And so they gave us food. You know, they made sure that, you know, again, they made sure that we don't overeat, and we eat the right food, and drink the right amount of water, you know? And we were being examined, you know? And we are being treated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- Did you feel you were treated well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yeah, you know, most of us, you know, we were sick. You know, we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Undernourished, yeah, undernourished.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you sick? How much did you weigh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, who knows? But I didn't weigh very much. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like how much?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea in those days, no. And probably, I was probably there-- I don't remember-- maybe two months, roughly speaking. Then they asked people if they want to go back to their-- to their home. And they made up transports to go home. And I said-- decided, yes, I-- I want to go home. And we went to-- we passed through Czechoslovakia, Prague. And they gave us food. But we didn't stay there. We kept on going. But we did stop in Slovakia, in Bratislava. And they gave us a-- a hotel. You know, we stayed in a hotel. And we-- we were there a few days. And that's where I met my brother. And it wasn't me, actually--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you see? How did you find him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was-- there was a couple of girls from my hometown. And going in from the hotel into downtown, they saw him. And they say-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH]. His name is [NON-ENGLISH]. He says, hey! Your brother is with us. He-- he-- come-- come with me to the hotel. And they brought him over. And-- and then on the way, when I was going back to the hotel-- people were going back and forth, back and forth-- so somebody else from that group went to downtown, and they saw me. He says, you go into our room, and we have a surprise for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I say, well, what is the surprise? Oh, I'm not gonna tell you. And I forced-- I forced her to tell me. And she said, your brother is waiting for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we met. And we kissed, and we hugged. And we stayed together and then went back home. So the first thing you have to do is you have to register with them for an ID. Register with whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: With-- with the-- with the-- with-- with the police. Yeah. And they gave you an ID. So they say, well, we gonna give you a week to settle yourself down. Find yourself a place where you're gonna live. And then you come back over here and report for work. We're gonna to give you a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are these the-- what nationality are these police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that-- that was-- that was-- that was at that time, it was under Russia-- Russian occupation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said, yes, sir. Yes, sir. As soon as we walk out of there, I said to my brother, I say, we're not gonna stay here very long. We gonna be leaving. So even so, my brother is two years older, but I took charge. Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: --because I-- I felt that I-- I am more capable. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- Do you know why?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you feel that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- how shall I say this? He-- I saw that he was more-- he-- he got more affected by the war. And his thinking was not as clear as mine. So I knew that I had to take charge. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: How old I was at that time? I was 17 going on 18. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, well, where are we gonna go? I says, first, we're gonna go to Budapest. And then we'll go to-- we'll go to Palestine. That was even before it-- Israel became a state, you know. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was Palestine. We had-- we couldn't think of going to America, because we didn't have anybody here. And-- and you to go to America, somebody have to send you a visa. So the only other place was left was Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your mother and your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't come back. They didn't come back. I did meet somebody when I got home to Munkatch. I met somebody who was in the same camp with my father. And what he told me is what happened that he got bitten by an SS dog at-- on the work-- you know, the place of work. And he was sent-- they couldn't take care of him there. It was a small camp. So they send him to Dachau. And that was the last time they saw him. So he-- he died over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mother, I don't know what exactly whether-- how soon after she died. Nobody-- nobody ever told me that she wound up in the camp first or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was Munkatch like after the war? Could you recognize it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Well, the-- the train station was bombed a little bit. And also the bridge was bombed. We had a nice bridge, and that was bombed. And I think that's about the only thing that was bombed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: My home, somebody else was living there already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to look at it, yeah. But I didn't want to stay there, anyway. I didn't want to stay there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were all your things?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't go inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I just passed-- just passed by, you know? There's a-- the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What stopped you from going inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I felt that it's-- it's-- it's no longer my home. And I knew that I'm not gonna make this my home, this city. I want out. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we did go to Budapest. Were there any Jews left in Munkatch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, there were some Jews left. I mean, you know, they went home. And they remained there, what I'm trying to say. They didn't make--

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- they went back, and they made a--

speaker: interviewee
text: --they went back, and they made it their home, yes. They started to build a life again. Yeah. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Many?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. But I-- I-- I do know-- I do know one fella, that he is-- he is here now back, 21-- he-- he's here 21 years. So he stayed there about approximately about 25 years or so-- 25, 26 years after the war. And he said there's a lot of people. They-- they came back, and they got married. And they had children. And then they lived there. But I didn't-- I didn't want to stay. I said, I'm not gonna stay here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing to stay there for. As a matter of fact-- so in Budapest, when we got to Budapest, we joined-- we joined a kibbutz. And they made up transports to go to Palestine. And everything went illegally, you know. And some of the ships-- there were some ships that were sunk. Others were captured and taken to Cyprus.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened to me is, first of all, the-- the people who made up the transports, they separated me and my brother. They say-- they say, because, you see, I-- I understand now them-- the reason for it is because if something should happen, one should remain alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said to me-- he says, you go first. That our-- that our family shouldn't disappear.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, right. This is what I hear. This is what I understood. Actually, nobody told me this. But I-- I figured that out.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the sense you made of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they sent me first. And I went as far as Austria. And from Austria, we were supposed to go to-- to Italy. And from Italy, we were supposed to go to Palestine. So I waited and waited. It was a long procedure. And one day, I see a bulletin-- 'Children under 18 should register.' And they'll take 'em to America. But belonging to a kibbutz and going to meetings, you become indoctrinated to go to Palestine, to build a homeland. So when I saw the-- that bulletin, I didn't rush right away to register. And in fact, the thought occurred to me to tear that down, that other children my age and younger shouldn't see it-- shouldn't be tempted to go to America. But instead, what I-- I didn't tear it down. That evening, when I went to sleep, I was doing some thinking. I says, who knows how much longer am I gonna have to be here before I can go? And I wanted to get out of Europe in the worst way.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's when I made up my mind. I'm gonna register. But with this in mind, wherever is gonna come up first, that's where I will go, as long as I get out of here. So it came up for me to go to America first.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is-- so I-- I-- I wind up over here. And it took me-- from the time we separated in Budapest, with my brother-- it took me five years to get in touch with him again. And so I wind up-- I lived in Pittsburgh. And one day, I see-- I was reading a magazine-- the Jewish-- called the It's a Jewish magazine, you know? There was an ad, children, I mean, people who are still looking for-- for a family should write to New York to this and this address. And I wrote.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I gave all the information about my brother. And at first, I used to get letters from people-- not my brother. Everybody wanted to have a brother in America. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that they weren't your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I asked-- I wrote them back, and I asked questions-- never heard from them again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I received a letter from--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of questions did you ask?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I asked parents' name and where did they live-- never received an answer. Then I received a letter from my brother. I knew right away it was him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just had a feeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Feeling-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And you know, of course, I wrote him back and I asked questions. And I got all the answers I needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I-- I worked in a wholesale men's haberdashery store in Pittsburgh. And there were three partners. But I worked on the inside. I was the inside man with-- with one of the partners. And they liked me so much that they were willing to send an affidavit to my brother, to bring him over-- send him as a-- as a relative, as a cousin. You know what I mean? Why not as a brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my boss claimed to be a relative

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, of him. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That this was his cousin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes. So-- but my brother, at that time, was just released from the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was in Israel, your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in Israel, yeah. And he was in the reserves.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was approximately 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, after the War of Independence.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So-- and Israel needed the young people, especially-- especially somebody who already served in the army in the-- in the reserves. So they made 'em very, very difficult to get out. In the meantime, he got married. And he married a girl from Iraq. And she had a lot of family over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what are you gonna do? If he gonna to bring 'em both over, then she is going to be separated from her family. So we all decided that it's best that you stay where you are, and I stay where I am. And I'll come and visit you. So I was there already three times.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, so-- so-- then-- so he remained over there. And you know, I just send them-- I send them packages, send him what he needs and help them out. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do? What happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened to me is I decided, as much as I liked the people I worked for, and they liked me, and I had a good job-- I became an inside salesman-- and filling the orders. It was a wholesale men's haberdashery store over there. And I met up with some other friends. And we talked it over that we'll go to California-- go to Los Angeles. So we bought a car together, and we drove across country.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one of them is Eddie, Eddie [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we're still very good friends. Yeah. And we find-- you know, worked on jobs, different jobs, and worked as a-- the first thing, we got a job as a-- door-to-door, selling dishes and silverware. But I-- my conscience was bothering me that they charged too much money and interest on the balance. So I just quit that job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I worked for a-- other wholesale stores, you know, a haberdashery, and clothing, and costume jewelry. Then I find a job. Then I see an ad in the paper where Douglas Aircraft was hiring. So I made up an -- filled out an application, and I-- I got hired. As a--

speaker: interviewee
text: As a stock clerk. So I worked as a stock clerk and then got into different classifications-- expediter, and this and that, you know. And then McDonnell Douglas-- then Douglas Aircraft got into financial trouble, so they merged with McDonnell from St. Louis. So that's when it became McDonnell Douglas.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stayed there until I retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you-- did you start dating?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes, of course, I started dating. I always used to love to dance. So I do-- I used to go ballroom dancing. And that's where I met my ex-wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married in December of 1957.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any children from that marriage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- one daughter from that marriage. And my daughter is now married, happily married. She's got a good husband. He is from Canada. And they have a 9-year-old boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Jason Helen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jason. And her name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter's name is Karen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Karen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do they live in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: They live in the Valley, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so that brings us up to date.

speaker: interviewee
text: That brings us up to-- pretty much to date, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. One question to ask you-- what do you think kept you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: What kept me going?

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me a little, but what kept you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the-- in the concentration camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what kept me going is that you-- one thing you had to do is you had to have hope that some day this is gonna come to an end. Because once you have nothing to hope for, then you have nothing to live for. And you'll-- you just fall apart. You're a goner.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you kept that hope.

speaker: interviewee
text: I kept that hope. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah. 'Cause I-- I've seen-- I've seen people-- strong, big people-- and once they gave up hope, they-- they-- they're gone. They fell apart.
